feed.title: Combined summary - [Research] Bitcoin After Block Rewards : preventing miner's deviation when the Bitcoin rewards is zero

entry.title: Combined summary - [Research] Bitcoin After Block Rewards : preventing miner's deviation when the Bitcoin rewards is zero
entry.summary: Junhyuk Lee's research paper addresses the crucial issue of Bitcoin's network security as it transitions away from block rewards, a situation expected to fully manifest by 2140. The study critically examines how Bitcoin's security protocol might adapt when miner compensation is solely derived from transaction fees. Lee proposes a threshold condition, termed G_t, to determine when miners might deviate from their prescribed roles due to diminishing block rewards. The paper reveals that significant deviations among miners can occur even at relatively low fee levels, specifically when fees constitute only 0.17% of transaction values, posing a substantial risk to network integrity in a fee-only regime. Furthermore, Lee’s paper evaluates several strategies to counteract the potential decrease in miner profitability and consequent security risks. These include implementing a floor fee compatible with soft-forking and combining tail emission with an EIP-1559 style fee burn and priority tipping. This combination could potentially offer a balanced solution by addressing different aspects of transaction fees and sustainability within the blockchain network. The original content discussing these concepts in depth can be accessed [here](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/addressing-the-diminishing-block-subsidy/2640). The feedback to the paper, though lengthy and constructed via AI assistance, brings forth additional perspectives on enhancing the model proposed by Lee. It suggests that the current static model of hash power may not realistically capture the dynamic nature of mining operations in a post-subsidy scenario. The critic underscores the necessity of incorporating a dynamic model of mining capacity, possibly including a dark capacity that is available but not constantly active, which could more accurately reflect real-world conditions. Additionally, the response highlights that securing the network should consider not just the cost of creating blocks but also ensuring these blocks are recognized and trusted across the network, especially in scenarios where Sybil attacks become feasible due to lower costs of block production. In summary, while Lee’s foundational work establishes a framework for understanding and addressing the future security implications of a subsidy-free Bitcoin network, the critiques received suggest areas for further refinement. These include broadening the assumptions about miner behavior and network dynamics, and explicitly modeling threats like Sybil attacks, which could significantly influence network security in a landscape dominated by transaction fees alone. Such enhancements could deepen the understanding of Bitcoin’s resilience and adaptability in facing diminishing block rewards.
